speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 2, an interview with Mr. Henry Laurant. We were talking about Habonim and the Zionist movement in Konigsberg and that somehow, it helped you get through the difficult period. Do you want to describe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, with pleasure. The, the, the thing it gave me was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a kind of-- I guess the current-- currently fashionable word is 'self-esteem.' That isn't quite right because I, I never had any lacking self-esteem. But it made my esteem clearer to myself. You esteem yourself as what? As what you are. And since I didn't quite know what I was, since I was surrounded by all these several, and, and often contradictory, facets, such as religious, who are you religiously; who are you as a national; who are you in-- as a-- as a-- as a-- as a-- at this stage in your life, et cetera.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this, and especially the Jewish identity, had been kind of nebulous, due to the very unreligious attitude and-- I wouldn't say un-Jewish attitude. My parents were always [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] conscious of being Jews. They just didn't do anything about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were [BACKGROUND NOISE] unreligious or areligious by-- through intellectual reasoning, which really made it very hard for them to have a faith. And they didn't-- were not able to somehow link the sense of Jewishness that they did have with the religious-- with the religious sense of Jewish religion that they did not have.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was a conflict there. And I was kind of caught in-between, admiring as I did the, the qualities of both my, my parents. I respected the, the areligiousness, non-religiousness.

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't anti-religious. It was just for them. They didn't tell anybody else what to do. They felt that way. But I didn't necessarily say that I would follow it. I respected somewhat less the degree of their assimilation into German life because-- somewhat less. Because I was suffering the consequences in a way, since I was being sent to a German-- to a German school and having the experiences that I just mentioned. So there was a bit-- little less enthusiasm about that and less understanding about that. And that's where the conflict came in. I really had to establish myself, even at that early age, as someone that had certain ideas, that had certain ideals as well. And I was in search of them. And Habonim was one of the-- and Zionism in general was one of the places where I went in search of it. Synagogue was another one. And the atmosphere of the synagogue immediately, it was a rather-- architecturally, a rather imposing and, and pleasant building. And it was there that I-- there, there was a-- the kind of-- the kind of atmosphere communicated itself to me that is supposed to come to you in synagogues, churches, et cetera. The builders of the synagogue would have been very pleased.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall how you made the connection, the Jewish connection? I mean, how, how did it come about?

speaker: interviewee
text: It came about, I'm afraid, in a negative way, no less strong. It came about through the experiences that I just mentioned, which made me understand my 'apart-ness' was the only way that I could-- that I could [BACKGROUND NOISE] explain the behavior of the other children, of the masters, of the world around me. I was starting to read newspapers [LAUGHS] at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I knew what was going on. It, it, it sounds very different from the typical high school child in, in, in, in America or probably in England, too. But, but under those circumstances, one, one grows up very fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: What I was curious about was, since it didn't come through your parents, did it come through students that you associated with, or-- I mean, how did you know where the Habonim were and the Zionist movement were?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had many friends. The friends had children. But I don't recall even that it was the children that-- through whom I got this information. It was, I think, the parents because I talked with the parents. They, understanding that I was a somewhat more grown-up child, spoke to me accordingly. And I asked questions. That was the one-- the one saving grace in this whole situation, that I had somebody I can ask questions of besides my parents, who, in that sense, were not too, too helpful, not, not-- through none of their fault. And so we had-- for example, for Seder, this was a-- at Passover, we-- it was a tradition. We didn't have Passover at our house. But we went to the house of very close friends, very close family of friends. And that is where I learned the-- that particular tradition, and so on. With, with-- by being in a context, in a social context, that was not-- certainly not entirely, but to a large extent-- to a sufficiently large extent-- Jewish, it helped me to get answers to the questions, find out where the Habonim is, find out what a Zionist was, find out what my parents were, politically speaking. There was-- they were referred to by one friend as a-- my father was referred to as a CV, which is not 'curriculum vitae.' What it stood for was Centralverein, which is Central Association of Jews in Germany. And that was the conservative branch, the non-Zionist branch, the assimilationist branch, which was very powerful politically. And, and then there were the Zionist-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Zionist wing. I don't know what the adult organizations were. But I knew about the youth organizations, Maccabi and the Habonim.

speaker: interviewer
text: As you think back, do you believe that the assimilation concept was a, a function of Nazism, or you don't think it had anything at all to do with--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --with Hitler.

speaker: interviewee
text: It had not anything to do with that, I'm afraid. It, it, it had a-- [SIGHS] I, I was a child. So I must go back to the testimony of an uncle of mine, a relative who was grown-up at-- in the '20s, and who, many years later, I had some long conversations with when he was-- worked for the Israeli government, living for a long time already in Israel. And he was their chief negotiator with the German government in Bonn on the compensation question and working all those things out with them. And we had dinner. I was living in Switzerland at the time. This was in the early '70s or late '60s. And, and I got an eye-opener about assimilation, about this very question that Jews had, had to fight very hard for, for assimilation, for becoming citizens, for equality. They never got equality. They did get citizenship in the-- in the 1900s. But there was always discrimination. And it was more or less accepted.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the assimilation was actually something that they had to hold-- they had something to hold onto culturally. And ideally, of course, it would have been very nice if they-- you can't blame them for not wanting to be apart from their host country completely.

speaker: interviewee
text: Besides, usually the arts, even the professions, to some extent, were the people where you-- were the-- were the areas where you found the more enlightened or the less unenlightened people in-- than, than in, for example, the bureaucratic class or the, the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] other, other different economic classes, which were not as well-educated, and therefore, were far more willing to follow people like Hitler later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the, the assimilation question is very complicated. And I can't hope to, to, to shed much light on it here. But one thing I can say-- it started at the-- about mid-19th century-- because how could it start before they had more or less, at least on paper, equal rights?

speaker: interviewee
text: They couldn't change their name, for example. Now, they could change their name if they wish. The, the-- they weren't admitted to many professions. Not generally known that in, in Germany before the beginning of the 19th century, no Jew was able to have a, a shop. Only they were able to be itinerant traders with their shop. Their shop was the tray of their goods that they carried in front of them. This was outside the ghetto. Inside the ghetto, they could-- they could have their shops. But the, the fact is that there was a lot going on in Germany culturally. And the temptation was very great. It's not maybe generally known that Richard Wagner-- Richard Wagner was a terrible anti-Semite. And his best and favorite conductor was Hermann Levi, L-E-V-I, a Jew. Wagner subjected Levi to indignities. But he always knew where to stop because he didn't want to lose his conductor. He knew that this was the best man. Well, they had many fallings-out, and they had reconciliations, et cetera, et cetera. But the psychology of the assimilated Jew is nowhere as well-- as well illustrated as in that kind of situation, where Levi, for example, I'm sure despised Wagner as a person, but as a composer, regarded him as a great composer. So he had to make a choice. And so on with many others that assimilated in that sense.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father serve in the German army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he did. In the First World War, he-- at that time, they had a separate army for the Bavarian-- Kingdom of Bavaria. And I guess the German emperor was also King of Bavaria. But they had a separate, separate unit and a separate army corps. And he was in that. But it's all, all the same thing, for our purposes. And he fought on the Western Front. And he was an officer. As a doctor, he became an officer automatically. And apparently, he, he was quite valiant and was promoted and got decorations and stuff. He was wounded. He was taken prisoner. He was exchanged in an officer exchange. So he was-- he got his-- he got his war experience, [LAUGHS] all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was, was he in any way rewarded for that later on as Hitler took over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, 'rewarded,' I wouldn't put it as generously as that. But after Kristallnacht, there was a new regulation that came out, along with many others, that no Jews were allowed to practice as doctors anymore. No lawyers were allowed to have law practices, Jewish lawyers. Knowing, however-- I mean, with, with patients or clients, with Germans-- Aryan, as the word had been coined by that time-- non-Jewish Germans as patients or clients. But the-- most of them were not allowed to practice, no matter what the religion of the client was, or the patient. But they knew that even the Jews had to have some medical treatment, or they'll [LAUGHS] simply start an epidemic, which would be bad for the general population. And they needed lawyers in order to-- for obvious purposes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they gave the-- they, they made a category out of what they called Frontkampfer, which was fighters at the front of the First World War, which was, at that time, the most recent war. And then they made all kinds of other things with decoration and ranks you'd got, et cetera, et cetera.

speaker: interviewee
text: And once you qualified and you got into that group-- and they have a limited number, therefore, that was able to practice-- doctors, a number of doctors-- was able to practice, but only treating Jews as patients-- same with lawyers. And my father became one of those-- of those doctors.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you live in Konigsberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: For 12 years, from birth until I was 12 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in 19-- that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: 1936, that's when you left Konigsberg? And what was the purpose for leaving Konigsberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: The purpose was that anti-Semitism had become worse and worse. It was a small town-- relatively small town of 300,000-- provincial capital. East Prussians are well-known for a certain quality-- in the rest of Germany, even-- for a certain quality, which is hardheadedness, sort of farmers, not sophisticated at all. And they were very ready, I would say, customers for the crap that, that the Nazi propaganda was feeding them. As a result, the, the anti-Semitic situation was somewhat worse there than in many other areas of the-- of the country. I can't compare it with any specific regions. It was bad. And I will never forget the-- I think the first time that my father really had it brought home to him-- and I mean home-- what the situation was was at the end of a-- for Whitsuntide, for, for Pfingsten, as it's called. This, this, this was a popular holiday, same as Easter. And people had, had-- it was a national holiday. And Pfingsten, usually what you did, you went out into the countryside, and you came back with green branches. It was a kind of a spring holiday, beginning of summer. And it was in May. And you came back, and you decorated your house or your apartment with these branches.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we, we had been out in the car. And we came back. And we had this open car that was seen on the-- on-- the convertible that you'd seen on the photo. And we drew up in front of our house-- let us out. And then my father would drive into the garage after we'd got out, my, my mother and sister and I.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got out, and I wasn't looking at the house. I was looking at something else on this side. There was the house. And I saw my father-- my father's face turned to stone. And I-- he was looking at the house. I looked at the house. And, as I mentioned before, his practice was in the-- in the apartment. So there was his professional sign-- it was copper, or whatever they, they, they have-- as a doctor was, was right fixed and in front of the door-- by, by the side of the door. And across the entire door and the entire-- of course, the-- the-- the sign itself and the whole front of the house was splattered with this 'Jude' and various other slogans, 'Juden raus,' and, and all kinds of really nasty things. And I mean, this, this was-- I will never forget my father's face when he saw that. This must have been like a-- like a dagger in his-- in his heart. And he must have intellectually-- as I said, he was an exceptionally intelligent man-- he must have intellectually known something like that was coming. It was inevitable. Even, even the most assimilated would be, at some point, hit with this. How far it would go, of course, nobody knew at that time. But this was the first time it really came home to him! That was his sign, his name besmirched by these vermin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what approximate date was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: '34 or '35, fairly shortly after the Nazis came to power in '33. It was at that time that he met-- I'll now go on to answer your question, why did we leave Konigsberg and went to Berlin? At that time, he met a disciple of Karl Gustav Jung, the, the Swiss psychiatrist, and through him, met Jung himself. And he had been getting more and more interested in psychoanalysis and psychiatry. And apparently, they-- this whole group made a big impression on each other. And he was invited to, to join Jung's group as a-- not, not on a permanent basis, but to take part in certain courses and certain seminars, and so on. And the other gentleman who was-- had been a disciple of Jung. And he was a German Jew and had opened his practice, had had a psychoanalytic practice in Berlin for quite some time, a very brilliant one. The man was quite brilliant. He became a very close friend of my father's. He ended up psychoanalyzing him. And so that, one way or another-- I don't know the details-- but through a combination of the analysis of specialized courses, of seminars with Jung, of doing papers with Jung, et cetera, et cetera-- and, and a very small, elite kind of groups of other people that were aspiring, aspiring psychoanalytic-- and, of course, others that were already in the field. This was a quite young branch of psychoanalysis at that time, you must remember, not like the Freudian one, which was really ruling the roost at that time. So, to cut a long story short, by '36, he was ready to open a-- sorry, to start himself in a psychoanalytic practice. [LAUGHS] He couldn't do that in Konigsberg. The only place where he could possibly do that in Germany would be Berlin. And so we moved to Berlin. And he did exactly that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so there was a dual purpose to his leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a dual purpose because the other purpose that certainly was a contributing factor was the treatment that we children-- my sister and I-- were getting in the school. The-- I wasn't able to hide my bloodiness-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --all the time. And my parents got a pretty good idea as to what was-- what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the friend of Dr. Jung? [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Franz Grunbaum, G-R-U-N-B-A-U-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a prominent psychiatrist at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you moved to Berlin, was there no sense that Berlin was equally as bad for Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, [SIGHS] there was-- not that you moved from pure black into pure white, no. But it was far, far, far better than Konigsberg for various reasons. One reason was that [SIGHS] the Olympic Games were slated to start within a couple of months. We moved there in April '36. So all, all the Nazis and everybody was on their very best behavior. That's an old story. I don't have to go to that much further. It's well-known. Secondly, they had-- my parents had made arrangements for my sister and myself to not again go into a public high school, but rather, to go into a privatized school that was-- had been opened for a couple of years before that by a very enterprising lady, who said to herself, Jewish children are having a miserable time there in the-- in the German schools. On the other hand, Jewish teachers are being fired-- all the best of the crop of the-- of the teachers are being fired because they're Jews. And I will pick the best of the best. And I will open a school with them. And on the one hand, the children will be happier. The parents will be happier. And I will be happier because I'll make a crock of money. [LAUGHS] You know, that's fine-- enlightened self-interest, nothing wrong with it. And I, for one, am most grateful to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Goldschmidt, Dr. Leonore Goldschmidt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it have a name, a-- the school itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Goldschmidt School.

speaker: interviewer
text: Goldschmidt School.

speaker: interviewee
text: Privatschule, private school, Dr. Leonore Goldschmidt. She was the pioneer. There were apparently two others that were somewhat smaller. But they followed the same-- the same system, the same principle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when the school was created? Was it in '36 or earlier?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in-- it must have been '34, '35. It was already off and running when we got there, which was '36. It was well-established. So it must have been one or two years before that. But obviously, not before '33 because before Hitler, there wouldn't be the incentive to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just before we leave Konigsberg, what was the approximate Jewish population of Konigsberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wish I could tell you. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a small community?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- the total population, as I think I said, was 300,000. Of that, if one takes the same percentage, let us say, of Jews in Germany to the Jewish population, that would be not far from 8%, as a matter of fact--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --7% or --7% Or 8%. So if we take the same percentage, it would be-- seven times three-- about 20,000. I think it was probably somewhat more than that. I would guess-- a very, very rough guess-- 20,000 to 30,000. But you must also remember that I knew a certain group of Jews, which was mainly the conservative ones or the non-religious ones, and in a certain class. There was also-- there were several Orthodox synagogues. There were also some Reformed synagogues. And so there may have been far, far more people than I-- than I was aware of. And there was also a constant influx of Polish Jews even long before the German occupation-- the Germans marched into Poland. Because Poles have not been particularly loving toward their Jews for quite a few years before Hitler. And so there were many that sought their fortunes and, and their-- and better treatment in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your, your, your father-- how was he able financially to take a tremendous loss, obviously, because he lost his medical practice? Was he a wealthy person, that he could afford to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was not wealthy by-- his family was not a wealthy family. But he had had a very successful and, I imagine, lucrative practice during quite a few years, certainly since the-- since the war, since the Second-- the First World War. So that's what, 15 years or so, between say '18 and, and '33, actually, '36, so even more-- close to 20 years. And I can only surmise that he had-- that he had some-- quite a bit of savings. What I can also vouch for is that not much-- very, very little remained at the end. But you're right, there was-- of course, he gave up one very lucrative practice in order to go into the unknown, really, into a new field, new for him, relatively new for the-- for the audience, for the-- for the public. And it was-- it was a very risky thing. But he was convinced that this was the thing to do. And, and-- there was-- he really felt that he had no choice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the house you moved into in Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's-- that-- that's an interesting, interesting situation. Psychoanalysts are not [SIGHS] sought or gone to by poor people. Therefore, to have the kind of patients that can pay for your services, you have to keep up the kind of-- the kind of front that, that, that they expect. And so we moved into a-- into the most elegant part of Berlin, a huge apartment. It was, in fact-- it had been the apartment of Franz Grunbaum. And he was getting a divorce at that time. So he moved into a-- first, he stayed in a, in a few rooms in the apartment. And then he, he moved into, into a flat, another apartment nearby, a smaller apartment. And, and-- however, we didn't have-- we had nice furniture that we brought from Konigsberg. But it wasn't anything like as elegant as the-- as you would expect coming in from the-- from the-- from the street into this-- [? this ?] house and-- or the area. And we were not living in the way we were clothed, the way we ate, the way we-- generally, we had-- we were not living like rich people. But we lived, we, we, we resided, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in an expensive place, which must have cost him dearly. And-- but, but he had to establish himself. And in order to do that, he had to do that. We stayed there for two years. And afterwards, we-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] till '38-- and in early-- middle of '38, we moved to a more modest place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, he developed a, a practice in psychiatry then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there no--

speaker: interviewee
text: Much smaller than his other practice had been, much less lucrative.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there are no restrictions as far as the profession?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that time. No, the only restriction on ones-- I mean, there were many restrictions about entry to professions, entry to universities, et cetera, et cetera. But once you were-- once you had all that, you had your education, you had your profession. The one restriction that I can-- [LAUGHS] that I can remember is since the Nazis had this obsession about the, the, the race-- about the, the 'rapist race.' Jews were-- their dearest wish was to-- was to rape or to have intercourse with non-Jews. Jewish women weren't of that much interest. But the other was a-- was a kind of a victory over the Aryan race. And so, driven by this obsession, in 1935-- and I'm sure you know this. It was quite well-publicized abroad. The Party Congress passed a regulation that in no Jewish-- no Jewish household must employ any household help, employee that is less than 45 years-- female that is less than 45 years old. They figured at 45 years old, [LAUGHS] nobody would want-- not even the rapacious Jew. So we had a lady working for us that was quite a bit more than 45, and a very dear, dear person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father display openly, in any way, anger about the transition from Konigsberg to Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was full of hope at that time. And don't forget, this was in '36. And there were [SIGHS] many, many Jews still that thought this would pass.

speaker: interviewer
text: There had been anti-Semitic waves before, anti-Semitic governments before. And who was to know that this one would not only not go away, but would get radically worse in its attitude toward, toward Jews? This is end of tape number 2.
